mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the philosophy of science including scientific methodology, the status of scientific theory, and validation of theories (B).
Section A.4.b covers the revolution in natural philosophy by Bacon, Descartes, and Galileo, which is the Scientific Revolution.
Section A.2.a covers science in Greek civilization including protoscience, disciplined observation, and the Platonic vs. Aristotelian view of nature.
Galileo is named in A.4.b.i as a key figure in the radical reformulation of natural knowledge during the scientific revolution.
Newton's work is foundational to A.4 and B.2.c-d on the Kantian interpretation of Newtonian physics.
The Copernican revolution is part of the rebirth of science in the Renaissance (A.4.a).
Descartes is named in A.4.b.i and B.2.b as central to the revolution in natural philosophy and the debate about methodology.
Aristotle's empirical approach and view of nature are discussed in A.2.a.ii and B.2.a on the Aristotelian approach to science.
Darwin's theory profoundly transformed 19th-century science (A.6) and raised enduring questions in the philosophy of science.
The history of physics from Copernicus through Einstein is a major component of the history of science throughout section A.
Chemistry's development is part of the history of science in section A, including institutional organization of science.
Medicine's history is part of the broader development of science in A.5-A.7, including social organization aspects.
The development of mathematics is intertwined with the history of science, especially the mathematical formulation discussed in B.3.a.
Section B.6 addresses the relevance of scientific knowledge to other spheres of human experience, including science-religion tensions.
The demarcation between science and pseudoscience is central to the philosophy of science, relating to B.3 and B.4 on validation.
Section B.2.e covers responses to relativity, quantum mechanics, and changes in natural sciences, core to philosophy of physics.
Section B.5.b discusses applying different philosophical doctrines to different sciences; biology raises unique philosophical questions.
Faraday exemplifies 19th-century experimental science and the progress in physics discussed in A.6.
Bohr's quantum mechanics is central to the profound changes driving 20th-century philosophy of science debates (B.2.e).
Hume's empiricism is central to B.2.c on 18th-century philosophy of science and the problem of induction.
Section B.2.c-d covers Kantian interpretation of Newtonian physics and belief in the unique rationality of the classical synthesis.
Section A.5.b covers the intellectual origins of revolution in the spirit of the Enlightenment.
Objectivity is a central concern of the philosophy of science, relating to B.3-4 on empirical procedures, validation, and scientific theory.
Causation is fundamental to scientific explanation, relevant to B.3 on empirical procedures and B.4 on scientific discovery.
Section B.3.b specifically lists measurement as a key empirical procedure of science.
Astronomy's development is a central thread in the history of science from ancient Greek through modern times.
Section B.2.e discusses relativity as one of the profound changes driving 20th-century debate in philosophy of science.
Section B.2.e discusses quantum mechanics as a profound change driving 20th-century philosophical debates.
Pavlov exemplifies the social organization and common pattern of advance in early 20th-century science (A.7).
Eugenics illustrates the moral and political difficulties facing science discussed in A.8 on contemporary problems.
The measurement of time is part of the history of science and its empirical methods throughout section A.
Section A.4.a.ii covers the influence of technology on science, and A.4.b.ii covers the results of the new philosophy in practical fields.
Section A.2.a.ii covers the Platonic view of nature and its contrast with the Aristotelian approach, foundational to philosophy of science.
Continental philosophy addresses positivism and phenomenology's relationship to science, relevant to B.2.e on 20th-century debates.
Montaigne's early modern skepticism relates to Renaissance-era coexistence of new discoveries and old philosophical views (A.4.a.iii).
